Comments: I do believe climate change is an important issue, but couldn't support all of the questions/points in the survey. It's not because I don't think change is needed, but it concerns me that the Government does not always have proper infrastructure for these changes. An example is electric cars, I know more is being done now, but for a good while the basic facilities, ie charging points, were not readily available across the country. The same with heat pumps, I just don't believe these are suitable for all homes. They are great in new builds where the plans accounted for them, but not so great in many older properties. While I don't have the answers on how to move forward, I don't agree with the government making demands on people that aren't always practical. That's on them to fix.

Comments: Causal link between CO2 and climate change has never been proved. Every prediction of climate calamity from the last 50 years has failed to become reality. Even if UK achieves net zero, a near impossible task, global CO2 output will be the reduced by a tiny amount for a crippling cost to taxpayers of hundreds of billions of pounds. Comments: The obsession with renewable energy fools many people into believing that renewables can at present replace fossil fuels. This is false. Without efficient, scalable energy storage [not yet available], 'renewable' is just another word for 'unreliable'. All civilisations need power available 24x7, which either means fossil fuels, or a well-planned collection of nuclear power plants, or a mixture of both. Climate change is certainly a problem, but even if all use of gas and oild were to be stopped overnight, the temperature rise would continue for at least a century, so some adaptation must be considered. Furthermore, until gross energy users such as India, the USA and China take climate change seriously, there is *no point* in smaller Western countries bankrupting themselves for no gain.

Comments: Climate change is real and needs to be tackled. I feel renewables are the wrong way to go. They are expensive, intermittent and unreliable. The UK already has the highest energy costs in the world. If we stopped all carbon emissions today in the UK, the world would experience barely a 1% drop in carbon. It would make no difference. However it would collapse the economy, cause mass starvation, death and political instability. We have to invest in new nuclear (fission) for now and utilise our local oil/gas supplies rather than importing. I hope that the new fusion reactors will solve the worlds energy problems in 20 years time, and remove the need for any renewables, gas, oil, and fission. With unlimited clean electricity and a hydrogen economy we should be able to rapidly reduce and control carbon emissions worldwide without sending local populations back to the dark ages.  Meanwhile, the bigger (short-term) threats are still nuclear war and global pandemics.

Comments:
Nothing specific! However, I do find personal grievance with JSO in particular - I find that they're a bit of a shell pressure-group. The lead of the group, if I remember correctly, is a direct family member of an oil billionaire.

I sometimes have wondered whether the actions of JSO have been purposefully orchestrated by those at top to portray a negative view of climate protesters, for the explicit purpose of painting such protesters as wrong/the enemy. Big Oil has done worst in the past - remember the phrase "carbon footprint"? I recommend looking into who coined and/or perpetuated the phrase strongly! Lot's of Big Oil involvement, if I remember rightly. That, and you can look at the many natural disasters, and human lives lost in third world countries, all in the name of black gold.

I do sincerely believe that those on the ground/in the field so-to-speak within JSO were genuine supporters of such a cause, but I would imagine some meddling was afoot. JSO had a strangely specific habit of going after things which would very obviously upset the general public - but not with good reason. Sometimes it'd be things that were not even worth the trouble. Unless someone was trying to paint environmentalists as bad - in which case, they did the perfect job.

Comments: What these people need to realise is that oil is used to make many items that we wear - for example, plastics, synthetic clothing, trainers, and tyres for cars, bikes. One of the protesters was wearing sunglasses - how does she suppose these were made (I assume they are made of plastic).  Heat pumps are a great idea, but if you live in a house that just cannot accommodate one, what then? What about all the terraced houses in this country where there are no private driveways for charging a vehicle.  Do we have pipes trailing across footpaths instead?  I think that would be a health and safety issue! The thing about climate change is that this planet we call Earth has been through numerous transformations over millions of years.  From the jungle forests that grew across Europe to the Ice Age that wiped everything out.  The planet evolves, and has been doing so for hundreds of years.  We are not within our capabilities to prevent those changes.  Whatever small changes we make in terms of climate change will never be enough to outdo the huge changes this planet goes through.  It's simply out of our control.
